---
title: "Private Repository & BOM Override"
tagline: "Or what to Do When You Need Enterprise Support"
type: scenario
tool: sbt
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When You Need Enterprise Support*
This scenario shows how to incorporate a private, third-party repository that provides patched or extended-support versions of dependencies. It specifically demonstrates how a third-party Bill of Materials (BOM) can be used to override a publicly available, EOL (End-of-Life) dependency with a patched version from the private repository.
This is a common enterprise pattern when a vendor provides long-term support for a library (like Spring Boot) after its official EOL date.

::: {.callout-note title="Tool Versions"}
This scenario was tested with **sbt 1.12**, which uses the **Coursier** resolver (the default since sbt 1.3). Under the older Ivy engine (sbt < 1.3, or `useCoursier := false`) resolution edge cases differ and settings like `conflictManager` behave differently. `dependencyTree` is built in since **sbt 1.4**; earlier versions need the sbt-dependency-graph plugin.
sbt 1.x has no native BOM import; **sbt 2.x** adds it.
:::

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    subgraph Public Repos
        SpringBoot["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14 (EOL)"]
    end
    subgraph Private Repo
        PatchedStarter["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14.ACME"]
        VendorBOM["com.thirdparty.springboot:patched-bom:1.0.0"]
        VendorBOM -. manages .-> PatchedStarter
    end
    Root[Project Root] -- imports --> VendorBOM
    Root --> SpringBoot_Declared("spring-boot-starter (version from BOM)")

```

#### ! Example
The `build.sbt` file first adds the private repository to the list of `resolvers`. Unlike Maven and Gradle, sbt 1.x has no native way to import a Maven BOM, so the vendor's BOM is emulated: its managed versions are pinned via `dependencyOverrides`, which forces the patched version even though the declared dependency asks for the public release. (For real BOM consumption, the [`here-sbt-bom`](https://github.com/heremaps/here-sbt-bom) plugin reads a Maven BOM and feeds it into `dependencyOverrides`; sbt 2.x adds native BOM support.)

```{.scala include="build.sbt" snippet="repo-and-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
A private repository plus BOM-derived version pins enable the build to automatically select vendor-patched versions over public EOL releases.

#### ! Why
SBT resolves custom artifacts via private `resolvers`, while `dependencyOverrides` forces the specific patched version everywhere in the graph — including direct declarations. The absence of native BOM import is one of the ways the same graph behaves differently across tools.

#### ! Check
**Fast check:** `sbt "show externalDependencyClasspath"`

**What to look for:** The classpath entry for `spring-boot-starter` resolves to `2.5.14.ACME` from the private repository, not the public `2.5.14`.

**Conclusion:** The override redirects resolution to the vendor-patched artifact.

#### ! Related
*   [SB17](../scenario-17-private-patch/guide.qmd) - Pinning a patched version directly instead of via a BOM.

#### ! Takeaway
Adding a repository makes its artifacts *available*, but it is the combination of the repository and a version control mechanism (like an imported BOM) that allows you to safely override public dependencies with private, patched versions.
